title: Efficient Batch Verification: Recent Progress and Challenges
speaker: Ron Rothblum
abstract:
Suppose Alice wants to convince Bob of the correctness of k NP statements. Alice could send the k witnesses to Bob, but as k grows the communication becomes prohibitive. Is it possible to convince Bob using smaller communication? This is the question of batch verification for NP statements.
In the talk, I will discuss recent progress on this question, showing different settings in which we can (and cannot) perform batch verification.

proof_stages.label: Batch-verification problem and desiderata
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates batch verification for k NP statements and contrasts witness-sending and the generic IP=PSPACE solution with the desired short communication, efficient honest prover, and zero-knowledge properties.

proof_stages.label: Prior doubly efficient batching for UP
proof_stages.summary: Existing work gives near-optimal, polynomial-time-prover batch interactive proofs for UP relations, making the main open question whether such doubly efficient batching can extend from UP to all NP.

proof_stages.label: Batch proofs force statistical witness indistinguishability
proof_stages.summary: A new theorem says that efficient public-coin batch proofs imply a statistical witness-indistinguishable proof for the same relation, with inverse-polynomial hiding error and a nonuniform honest prover, giving evidence against analogous batching for all NP.

proof_stages.label: Reduction proof via distributional hiding and min–max
proof_stages.summary: The proof first obtains distributional SWI by hiding the real instance among random samples and applying a compression lemma, then uses a sparse min–max theorem plus standard transformations to obtain worst-case SWI for interactive protocols.

proof_stages.label: Zero-knowledge batching target for SZK and NISZK
proof_stages.summary: The second half asks whether statistical zero-knowledge proofs can be batched without revealing witnesses, defines the common-randomness noninteractive SZK model, and states that every NISZK language has a short NISZK batch proof.

proof_stages.label: Complete-problem strategy for NISZK batching
proof_stages.summary: The construction moves from an easy permutation warm-up to an injectivity-type NISZK-complete problem, where naive composition must be replaced by composing the circuits with intervening random hashes.

proof_stages.label: Entropy gap and derandomized hashing
proof_stages.summary: The proof handles hash collisions by showing entropy loss stabilizes, measures the yes/no gap through Rényi entropy and collision probability, and derandomizes the many hash functions using bounded-space pseudorandom generators.

proof_stages.label: Combining efficiency and zero knowledge; open challenges
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by comparing the UP protocol and the NISZK protocol, noting recent work combining their advantages in some regimes, and listing open problems on NP obstructions, interactive SZK batching, overhead, and characterization.

examples.name: Batch verification of products of two primes
examples.note: The speaker repeatedly uses RSA-like integers that should factor as products of two primes to make the abstract batch-verification, UP, and zero-knowledge questions concrete; it motivates the setting but does not supply the proof idea.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the two-prime-product batching problem as the running instance, analyzes witnesses/factorizations and k·m communication, and applies the UP/SZK batching discussion to it

examples.name: Permutation-checking warm-up
examples.note: Working through the easy case where k permutation tests batch by composing the functions visibly produces the compression strategy that is then adapted to the NISZK-complete injectivity setting using hashing.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker develops the permutation-checking warm-up in detail, giving the CRS/inverse proof, checking completeness/soundness/zero knowledge, and batching it by composing functions

passes.reason: Permutation checking uses bijections, the maximum-image/no-collision regular maps, as an easy warm-up.
permutation checking uses bijections, which are collision-free finite maps with maximal image size and minimal fiber size, as a positive warm-up
